query: mechanism of circular RNA formation via back splicing

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Mechanism -- Splicing and Pre-mRNA Modifications
retrieved_docs.content: Splicing occurs within the nucleus. Several steps are catalyzed by large (60S) molecules called spliceosomes composed of small ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs) and splicing factors. These enzymes excise the introns from the mRNA transcript while leaving exons in the transcript alone. Spliceosomes shuffle around these exons, depending on the type of protein that needs to be synthesized. After splicing, the 5′ and 3′ tails are modified to help the translation process. At the 5′ end of the pre-mRNA molecule, GTP reacts with the first nucleotide in the triphosphate group on the 5′ ribose's carbon and form a 5′–5′ triphosphate linkage; the nitrogen-7 of guanine is then methylated to form the 5 ′ caps of the mRNA. At the 3′ end, the sequence AAUAAA is cleaved by an endonuclease so that a Poly(A)-polymerase can add the adenylate residues. After all the necessary modifications are made, the mRNA strand is considered mature and is then translocated to the cytoplasm to begin translation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Historically, pseudogenes were referred to as "junk DNA" due to their location in non-coding sequences of the genome. However, recent studies have begun to unravel various functions of pseudogenes and their mRNA transcripts. These functions vary in regulatory roles, and they include antisense RNA, siRNA, miRNA-like, and miRNA binding or inhibiting properties.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Molecular Level -- Transcription
retrieved_docs.content: Once synthesized, the RNA undergoes post-transcriptional modification to prevent its degradation during its exit from the nucleus to the cytoplasm where it will be translated to protein. The single-stranded RNA receives a 5' capping by 7-methylguanosine, which is often an mRNA sequence, as well as the addition of poly-A-tail on the 3' end. During these modifications, eukaryotic cells also undergo splicing, in which portions of the RNA called introns are cut out, leaving only the required bases for translation, known as exons. The newly synthesized RNA strand then travel through the pores in the nuclear envelope to the cytosol in order to participate in protein synthesis by the process known as translation. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of RNA translation into protein is carried out in three steps: initiation, elongation, and termination. During initiation, a tRNA and ribosome join an mRNA. The start codon, AUG, codes for methionine and will always be the first amino acid in the sequence. After initiation, the ribosome moves along the mRNA in the 5’-3’ direction, reading the codons and binding tRNAs carrying the respective amino acids for which the codons are coding. The sequence of amino acids progressively becomes longer and terminates upon reaching a stop codon. The stop codons are UAG, UAA, and UGA. The polypeptide is then released from the ribosomal complex and modified into an active protein. In eukaryotic cells, translation occurs after transcription, while in prokaryotic cells, transcription and translation occur simultaneously.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Transposons -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Transposons can be divided based on the mechanism of transposition and integration. Class I elements, retrotransposons, use an RNA intermediate that is reverse-transcribed into a cDNA copy that reintegrates into a new genomic home.  Class II elements are DNA transposons mobilized by a DNA intermediate or by replication involving a circular DNA intermediate. Each of these classes can further sub-categorize based on common genetic or monophyletic origins.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Proteins -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Translation

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Fundamentals -- mRNA
retrieved_docs.content: mRNA is transcribed from DNA and contains the genetic blueprint to make proteins. Prokaryotic mRNA does not need to be processed and can proceed to synthesize proteins immediately. In eukaryotes, a freshly transcribed RNA transcript is considered a pre-mRNA and needs to undergo maturation to form mRNA. A pre-mRNA contains non-coding and coding regions known as introns and exons, respectively. During pre-mRNA processing, the introns are spliced, and the exons are joined together. A 5’ cap known as 7-methylguanosine is added to the 5’ end of the RNA transcript, and the 3’ end is polyadenylated. Polyadenylation refers to the process where a poly(A) tail, which is a sequence of adenine nucleotides, is added to the transcript. The 5’ cap protects the mRNA from degradation, and the 3’ poly(A) tail contributes to the stability of mRNA and aids it in transport. Researchers are also studying mRNA as an anti-cancer treatment due to its ability to modify cells. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: After a mature mRNA transcript leaves the nucleus, a ribosome attaches to the transcript to begin translation. The initiation site in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes is not composed of a unique initiation sequence but is more probably a multisubstrate enzyme system based on substrate specificity. [5] [6] [7] [8] A postulated mechanism (scanning mechanism) predicts that the ribosomes initiate protein synthesis by recognizing and binding the 5′ tail of the mRNA corresponding to a 7-methylguanosine referred to as "cap." [9] The first methionine codon located in the 5′ ends of the mRNA is the initiator codon. Several factors bind to this initiation sequence (IF4F initiation factors), forming the ribosome-eIF2-GTP-Met-tRNA preinitiation complex (PIC). [10] A favorable initiation sequence in mammals is known as “Kozak consensus”: 5’ (A/G)CCAUGG 3’. The PIC scans the mRNA in the 5’ region until it reaches the AUG start codon complementary to Met-tRNA's anticodon. The first AUG codon (“leaky scanning”) can be skipped to use a downstream AUG. This creates protein isoforms when the downstream AUG is located in the same open reading frame as the first 1 or a completely different protein if it is not in-frame.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: A series of proteins called transcription factors aid RNA polymerases in their transcription function. These factors have several functions, mainly facilitating the binding of RNA polymerase II to the promoter sequence, forming the transcription initiation complex. The initiation complex recognizes the promoter region in the DNA as a signal for the transcription starting point. The consensus sequence TATTAA (called TATA box) is located 25–35 bases upstream of the initiation site, followed by a poly-G/C consensus sequence located 32–38 bases upstream. The transcribed RNA contains multiple segments called exons and introns. Exons are segments containing the encoding sequence for protein synthesis, while introns are intervening segments of nucleotides that are excised from the primary RNA transcript (pre-mRNA) in splicing. Splice sequences are found at the 5′ and 3′ tails of introns marked with the dinucleotide GU at its 5′ end and AG at the 3′ end. Alternative splicing is a mechanism used to create different isoforms of the same protein so that several protein isoforms can be created from a limited repertoire of genes. Alternative splicing is now known to deeply influence biological behavior (sex determination, cell differentiation, and programmed cell death). [3] The mechanism underlying alternative splicing is not fully understood yet, but histone modification signatures correlate with splice site switching via chromatin-binding proteins. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Mechanism -- Transcription
retrieved_docs.content: Transcription begins in the nucleus with the formation of the pre-initiation complex. First, transcription factor II D (TFIID) binds to the TATA box through the TATA-binding protein (TBP). Then 5 other transcription factors (TFIIA, TFIIB, TFIIE, TFIIF, AND TFIIH) and RNA polymerase II combine through a series of stages to form the pre-initiation complex. Specifically, TFIIH has a role in nucleotide excision repair and separating the opposing strands of double-stranded DNA. [14] This strand is read by RNA polymerase in the 3’-5’ direction and is transcribed in the 5’-3’ direction. During this process, RNA Pol II attaches complementary base pairs to the template strand: adenine on the DNA strand is paired with thymine, guanine with cytosine, and guanine with guanine. However, whenever this enzyme reaches a thymine base pair on the DNA strand, it substitutes the original adenine complement base pair with a uracil base pair. Concerning protein-encoding genes, elongation continues until the polymerase transcribes the AAUAAA and GU-rich sequence. Once both sequences have been transcribed, cleavage-polyadenylation specificity factor (CPSF) and cleavage stimulatory factor (CstF) bind to the AAUAAA and GU-rich sequences, respectively. Poly(A) Polymerase forms a complex with CPSF, CstF, nuclear poly(A) binding protein (PABP), and a few other proteins to catalyze the addition of the 3’ poly-A tail of the pre-mRNA strand. [15] This newly synthesized pre-mRNA strand undergoes post-translational modifications to prevent degradation while exiting the nucleus to enter the cytoplasm. At the 5’ end of the pre-mRNA strand, the 7-methylguanosine cap is added by guanyl transferase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudogenes can classify into nonprocessed or processed pseudogenes. Processed pseudogenes appear near their paralogous gene form through retrotransposition. The process of retrotransposition occurs through the reintegration of a reverse transcribed mRNA transcript (cDNA) at a new location within the genome. Unprocessed pseudogenes differ from processed forms by retaining their intron-exon structure. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: To initiate the process of bacterial transcription, sigma and core together first binds to double-stranded DNA. [21] [2] C-terminal domains of the alpha subunits both interact with the UP elements, Region 4.2 interactions with the -35, and Region 3.0 interacts with the extended -10. [2] These interactions between core and promoter DNA are very unstable and short-lived, forming a product called the unstable closed complex (RPc). [21] The thinking is that the double-stranded DNA lies across the face of the RNAP. RPc quickly transitions to the stable open complex (RPo) where the DNA unzips, bends about 90 degrees, and forms a bubble from -11/12 to about +5 relative to the +1 transcriptional start site. [21] [22] Along with DNA changes, there are also major protein conformational changes within polymerase: Region 1.1 moves downstream to enter that beta and beta prime channel and beta prime clamp undergoes large conformational changes, allowing the downstream DNA to be fully secured. [22] Nucleotide triphosphates then enter through the secondary channel of the core, leading to the synthesis of RNA and forming a complex called the initiating complex (RPi). These RNA products are very short and are also known as abortives. Once the abortives reach a certain size, they can ultimately push Region 3.2 out of its current position within the RNA exit channel. [23] This change thus opens up the RNA exit channel, leading to the release of both the sigma factor as well as RNA. Core polymerase continues moving along the template stranded DNA until it reaches the termination site. In Rho-dependent termination, a factor called Rho is responsible for disrupting the complex of RNAP/RNA/template DNA while in Rho-independent termination, a loop forms at the end of the RNA molecule, allowing for RNAP to fall off. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Patisiran -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: This RISC separates the strands of the siRNA sequence. One strand releases and the other remains bound to the RISC. Then, the bound strand of siRNA functions as a targeting sequence for the complementary mRNA sequence. The bound patisiran strand binds to the genetically conserved sequence located in the 3' untranslated region of the complementary mRNA encoding abnormal transthyretin and the alignment of the RISC complex with it. [1] Subsequently, transthyretin mRNA is cleaved to render it nonfunctional. The degradation of mRNA coding wild-type and mutant transthyretin, a process of post-transcriptional gene silencing, results in reduced levels of TTR protein in the serum and, ultimately, deposition of TTR protein, also called amyloid, in tissues. [2] [14] One copy of the siRNA target sequence can destroy multiple complementary transthyretin mRNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- Small Nuclear RNA
retrieved_docs.content: Small nuclear RNAs (snRNA) are non-coding RNAs that are responsible for splicing introns. The snRNAs join with proteins to form small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNP), which most commonly contain U1, U2, U4, U5, and U6 snRNA molecules. [12] Spliceosome assembly and activity begin once U1 of the snRNP binds a complementary sequence on the 5’ splice site of a pre-mRNA transcript. Introns are then removed from the pre-mRNA transcript by the spliceosome complex, and mature mRNA forms.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Synthesis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: As with initiation, elongation requires the use of non-ribosomal proteins known as elongation factors. Eukaryotic EF1A (eEF1A) forms ternary complexes with aminoacyl-tRNAs and GTP.  These ternary complexes enter the empty A site of the ribosome and if an appropriate codon-anticodon interaction forms between the incoming aminoacyl-tRNA and the codon in the A site, GTP will be hydrolyzed and eEF1A released. At this point, the peptidyl-transferase site of the ribosome catalyzes peptide bond formation as the free amino group of the incoming aminoacyl-tRNA attacks the ester bond linking the growing polypeptide to the tRNA in the ribosomal P site. The resultant uncharged tRNA occupying the P site moves to the E (exit) site and leaves the ribosome. The growing polypeptide chain previously in the P site is now elongated by one amino acid as it transfers to the aminoacyl-tRNA in the A site.  The peptidyl-tRNA in the A site is then translocated to back to the P site with the help of eEF2 and GTP. The A site is now empty, and the entire process is repeated over and over again as the ribosome moves down the mRNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: On a molecular level, pseudogenes have associations with several roles. Numerous studies have revealed that specific genes and their associated pseudogenes display regulatory roles in the cell. Some pseudogenes have exhibited antisense RNA properties, siRNA properties, and even an ability to affect mRNA stability. Through these molecular functions, pseudogene transcripts can modulate the number of parental copy transcripts expressed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen has a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, meaning that treatment with this antisense oligonucleotide leads to reduced synthesis of proteins in the body. This decrease in protein synthesis is a key aspect of mipomersen’s mechanism of action and contributes to its therapeutic and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 116
retrieved_docs.content: oligonucleotide therapy to its broader preparation group. Understanding these classifications, mechanisms (direct versus peripheral action), chemical families, formulation details, and safety relationships is essential for clinicians to select appropriate agents, anticipate therapeutic effects, and manage potential side effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1705
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serve as the parent class for the therapeutic agent nusinersen, indicating that nusinersen is a specific preparation within this broader category. Nusinersen, in turn, produces increased protein synthesis as a physiologic effect, reflecting its mechanism of action. Understanding that nusinersen belongs to the N [Preparations] family and that it induces increased protein synthesis helps clarify its role in clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 653
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals and neuraminidase inhibitors, direct acting antivirals are both subclasses of direct acting antivirals. Rimantadine is a member of the cyclic amine subclass and produces the physiologic effect known as Cellular Synthetic Activity Alteration, indicating that it alters cellular synthetic activity. Zanamivir is a member of the neuraminidase inhibitor subclass, placing it within the broader category of direct acting antivirals. These connections show how individual antiviral agents such as rimantadine and zanamivir are organized within specific antiviral classes and highlight rimantadine’s mechanism of altering cellular synthetic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitory agents are classified as hypoxanthine derivatives, indicating that they belong to the chemical class of hypoxanthine and function as inhibitors of poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase. This classification highlights the structural relationship between PARP inhibitory compounds and the hypoxanthine scaffold, which is relevant for understanding their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Anagrelide is a pharmacologic agent whose mechanism of action involves inhibition of phosphodiesterase enzymes; it functions as a phosphodiesterase inhibitor, a property that underlies its therapeutic effects in relevant clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1358
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists, as defined by their mechanism of action, function as antagonists of beta‑1 adrenergic receptors, a relationship that underlies their therapeutic effects in cardiovascular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect associated with both mupirocin and nusinersen. Mupirocin, an antimicrobial agent, and nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, each promote the up‑regulation of protein production as part of their pharmacologic actions, highlighting a shared mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 0 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: these entities and relationships delineate a comprehensive framework of antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drug classes, their structural families, mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, epidemiologic links, and relevant contraindications, thereby informing optimal clinical selection, prevention, and treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a category of pharmaceutical preparations that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. As members of this preparation class, nedokromil and nusinersen share a common formulation lineage. Nusinersen, in particular, is noted for its physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, a mechanism that underlies its therapeutic action. Recognizing that both drugs belong to the same N [Preparations] group while nusinersen uniquely promotes protein synthesis can inform clinicians about their relatedness and distinct pharmacologic profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 69
retrieved_docs.content: pharmacologic class, mechanism of action, and therapeutic context of each agent, particularly when selecting combination hormone products like ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE for hormone replacement or contraceptive purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 626
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir and cidofovir are both classified as members of the “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” group. Within this class, ganciclovir’s mechanism of action is identified as “Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors [MoA],” meaning it exerts antiviral effects by inhibiting the synthesis of nucleic acids. By belonging to the same nucleoside/nucleotide category, both ganciclovir and cidofovir share structural and functional properties that target viral replication through disruption of nucleic acid synthesis, a key principle in antiviral drug therapy.
